_meta.purpose: Remote-work tax residency checker (task 2) — Phase 1 data. The 'convenience of the employer' states: states that tax a nonresident remote worker as if every day were worked at the employer's in-state office, which can create double taxation with the worker's home state. Reciprocity and credit-for-taxes-paid live in the existing src/data/reciprocity.ts — not duplicated here.
_meta.phase: 1 — data, pending owner sign-off. Confirm each state against its Department of Revenue before Phase 2.
_meta.schema: code -> { name, applies, nuance, employer_necessity_exception, source, source_url, verify_status }

NY.name: New York
NY.nuance: The oldest and most aggressive convenience rule. A nonresident who teleworks outside NY is deemed to work at the employer's NY location (NY-source wages) unless the remote work is for the employer's necessity, not the employee's convenience.
NY.source: NY Dept of Taxation and Finance — TSB-M-06(5)I (convenience-of-employer test for telecommuters); pandemic follow-up TSB-M-20(1)I

PA.name: Pennsylvania
PA.nuance: Pennsylvania's convenience doctrine counts days worked outside PA as PA earnings when the remote work is for the employee's convenience, not the employer's necessity. A nonresident required to telework full-time from another state should not source income to PA.
PA.source: PA convenience-of-employer doctrine (RKL LLP analysis of PA DOR guidance); practitioner consensus 2026

DE.name: Delaware
DE.nuance: Delaware applies a full convenience rule: a nonresident of a DE employer is taxed as DE-source unless the remote work is for the employer's necessity.

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.nuance: Nebraska applies a convenience rule, but 2024 legislation limits it to nonresidents physically present in Nebraska for MORE than 7 days in the year. A fully remote worker who never travels to NE is not caught.
NE.source: Practitioner consensus 2026 on the 2024 legislative limitation

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.nuance: Connecticut applies a convenience rule; sources differ on whether it is full or limited to nonresidents whose home state itself has a convenience rule. Treat it as applying and confirm the specifics.
CT.source: Practitioner consensus 2026 (some sources describe a reciprocal design)

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.nuance: NOT treated as a current convenience state. Arkansas adopted a convenience rule as a temporary COVID measure (Feb 2020) that ENDED on 21 April 2021. Some 2026 blog summaries still list it, but the specific dated repeal is more credible — so this tool does not assert Arkansas as a convenience state.
AR.source: Dated repeal (temporary 2020-2021 rule ended 2021-04-21) vs conflicting 2026 blog claims — resolved conservatively

NJ.name: New Jersey
NJ.nuance: Enacted a convenience rule that applies only to nonresidents from states that impose their own convenience rule (mirrors CT). New Jersey publishes an FAQ on it.
NJ.source: NJ Division of Taxation — Convenience of the Employer Sourcing Rule FAQ (primary)

_owner_decision_2026_07_24.ship_posture: The canonical, well-documented convenience states are New York (the classic) plus Connecticut, Delaware, Nebraska, and Pennsylvania; New Jersey is DOR-verified. Assert the rule for these with the employer-necessity exception. Arkansas is commonly cited but kept behind the same 'confirm' caution. For ANY state not clearly identified, do not assert a convenience rule — show the owner-approved caution and point to the DOR / a professional.
_owner_decision_2026_07_24.approved_copy: This tool does not attempt to fully model the 'convenience of the employer' rule beyond New York and a small group of clearly identified states. For other jurisdictions, always confirm nonresident wage sourcing directly with the state Department of Revenue or a qualified professional.
_owner_decision_2026_07_24.employer_necessity_note: Surface prominently: if the employer requires the remote work for a genuine business reason (not employee convenience), the wages may not be re-sourced.

_owner_review_notes: NJ is confirmed against the state Division of Taxation FAQ (primary). NY/CT/DE/NE/PA are the well-documented core per practitioner guidance. AR is commonly listed but not DOR-confirmed here — kept behind the confirm caution.
NE's rule applies only after 7+ days physical presence in NE (2024 amendment) — the example of why the list needs annual re-checking.
This is a rules-map, not a tax computation — the tool outputs which rules apply and where double-taxation risk sits, then leans on the existing reciprocity + credit-for-taxes-paid data.
